quote: And what got you interested in looking at solar?
stewart_read: Tyke opens the verify section with the codex's highest-value question immediately after confirming the address. The open-ended framing — 'what got you interested' rather than 'are you interested' — is exactly what the codex calls for. It directly unlocked James's 90-second disclosure covering bill pain, roof need, neighbor comparison, pool pump experimentation, and self-sufficiency interest. This is a positive verify execution worth crediting.
coaching_implication: Show this moment to the rep as a positive anchor. The question worked exactly as designed. The coaching gap is not the question — it's what Tyke did with the disclosure that followed.

quote: it's hard to get ahead in life already. I don't need somebody making money on me and then me having to pay another $100 a month because that won't happen. I'll just default because I can't I can't make I can't afford that.
stewart_read: James discloses financial vulnerability — 'I'll just default' is not a rhetorical flourish, it's a real fear about affordability. Tyke's response ('so No. And that yeah. Those are all good concerns to have') is a generic acknowledgment that skips past the emotional weight entirely. The codex classifies this as a trust gift: the customer is telling you exactly what they're afraid of. The correct move is to acknowledge specifically before any technical reframe.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyke may have been deliberately moving past the emotional moment to get to the satellite reframe, which he delivers at [02:41]. Rejected — the satellite reframe addresses the neighbor's situation, not James's personal financial fear. The two are distinct and both needed a response.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play the clip from [01:44] to [02:00] and ask Tyke: 'What did James just tell you about himself?' The goal is to surface that 'I'll just default' is a vulnerability disclosure, not an objection to rebut. The codex move is: acknowledge specifically ('That's a real concern — you're not looking to add a bill, you're looking to replace one'), then bridge to the design as the tool that shows whether the math works for James specifically.

quote: his electric bill went down, and he also kinda got a a roof restoration or repair out of it, or new roof. And my wife and I are looking at having to spend money on a roof right now
stewart_read: James volunteers two facts in the same breath: his friend got a roof through solar, and he and his wife are facing roof costs right now. This is a live cross-sell signal — Ion does roofing in-house, both standalone and as solar-install prep. Tyke does not bridge to Ion's roofing capability here. He later says at [03:08] 'I'm more than happy to kinda take a look at that with our every roof option as well' — a partial acknowledgment — but it arrives after the roof topic has already passed and is hedged ('kinda,' 'every roof option' appears to be a transcription artifact). The codex template calls for an active bridge: 'Ion actually does roofing in-house — we could potentially do both for you.'
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyke may have been holding the roofing bridge for after the solar qualification was confirmed, to avoid overloading the early conversation. Partially supported — he does return to it at [03:08]. Rejected as sufficient because the [03:08] mention is passive and hedged, not a genuine bridge to the roofing team.
coaching_implication: Use the codex cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned template in the 1-on-1. The moment James said 'my wife and I are looking at having to spend money on a roof right now,' Ion had a roofing sale available regardless of whether solar qualified. Tyke needed to name it explicitly and offer to route it — not fold it into a soft aside about 'our every roof option.'

quote: I'm more than happy to kinda take a look at that with our every roof option as well.
stewart_read: This is Tyke's second pass at the roofing signal — a partial attempt that falls short of the codex template. The hedged phrasing ('kinda,' 'every roof option') and passive framing ('take a look at that') do not constitute a bridge to Ion's roofing team. The codex calls for naming the capability explicitly and offering to route: 'Ion does roofing in-house — want me to have our roofing team take a look?' Tyke's line acknowledges the topic exists but does not move it forward.
coaching_implication: Contrast this line against the codex template in the 1-on-1. The gap is not awareness — Tyke knows Ion does roofing — it's activation. 'More than happy to kinda take a look' is not a routing action. The manager should ask: 'What would it have sounded like to actually hand this off to the roofing team right here?' SESSION18_fcbf33c1 (Meg/Kelly) is the positive exemplar for this bridge.

quote: Is there anybody else with the title with you, or is it just you?
stewart_read: Tyke executes the title/co-owner verify item correctly and receives a clean answer: 'It's just me.' This confirms no spouse protocol was triggered — James is the sole decision-maker. Worth noting because it explains why the spouse_handling protocol is not a coaching gap on this call. The verify item was asked at the right point in the sequence and answered definitively.
coaching_implication: Credit this as correct procedure. In the 1-on-1, this is a positive anchor — Tyke asked the question cleanly and got the answer. No coaching action needed on this specific item.

stewart_read: James confirms $250/month to Edisto Electric. Tyke captures it — 'About $250. Perfect.' — and immediately pivots to the credit qualifier setup ('And then in order to qualify'). The bill is captured and dropped. The codex's capture pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip': the flip would use James's own number to reframe the utility as the expense solar replaces. That move never happens. The net metering concern surfaces immediately after, so the bill anchor is never activated as emotional leverage.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyke may have been moving to the qualifier before the flip because he was already uncertain about Edisto's net metering eligibility and didn't want to build emotional investment in a lead he might have to DQ. Partially supported by the sequence. Rejected as sufficient — the flip is precisely the tool that would have made the net metering conversation higher-stakes and worth escalating rather than soft-exiting.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, play [04:19]-[04:22] and ask: 'What could you have done with $250 before you moved on?' The codex flip for a high bill is to reframe the utility as the ongoing expense — 'That's $3,000 a year going to Edisto. The design tells us whether we can change that.' This is the moment that would have made the net metering uncertainty worth fighting for rather than soft-exiting on.

quote: I was gonna say, it looks like we might not be approved with Edisto. Let me double check here. They might not have a great net metering program, which would be the thing that would kind of, save you some money. Let me check with my design team. I don't wanna get too deep in the weeds for it yet.
stewart_read: Tyke surfaces a net metering uncertainty he cannot resolve, explains the mechanism to James ('the thing that would kind of save you some money'), and then exits the call on a soft callback rather than escalating to a supervisor or holding the lead. The codex's universal escape hatch is 'That's exactly the kind of question our specialist is built to answer' — not 'I'll check and call you back if we can help.' The soft exit on an unconfirmed DQ is the call's terminal failure: Tyke unilaterally closes a lead he wasn't certain was disqualified.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyke may have known from prior experience that Edisto Electric Cooperative does not participate in net metering and was being honest rather than stringing James along. Possible — but the phrasing 'might not be approved' and 'let me check with my design team' signals genuine uncertainty, not confirmed knowledge. If Tyke knew, the correct move was still to escalate to a supervisor for confirmation before exiting, not to soft-exit unilaterally.
coaching_implication: This is the call's primary coaching moment. The manager should ask: 'What's the protocol when you're not sure if a utility qualifies?' The answer is escalate to a supervisor or design team in real time — not promise a callback. A warm lead with $250/month bill pain, a roof need, and genuine enthusiasm for self-sufficiency should not exit on 'if we're able to help you, I'll give you a callback.' Even if Edisto ultimately DQs, the roofing sale was still available and was never routed.

quote: my my dad and I have kinda dabbled solar panels trying to run my pool pump and stuff. And it'd be something cool. Like, we live out in the country a little bit. So Yeah. If power outages happen, it'd be nice to be maybe a little self sufficient. In the daytime, we have power, if that's even a thing.
stewart_read: James volunteers three distinct enthusiasm signals in one breath: prior hands-on solar experimentation (pool pump), rural location with power outage exposure (battery backup interest), and a self-sufficiency motivation. The pool pump reference signals an existing electric load that solar could offset. The power outage comment is a direct opening for Tesla Powerwall — Ion's only battery product. Tyke does not surface the battery option at any point in the call.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, flag this as a missed Powerwall opening. James described exactly the use case — rural, power outages, daytime self-sufficiency — that the Tesla Powerwall is designed for. The setter's scope is to create curiosity, not sell the product: 'That's actually something our specialist covers — there's a battery backup option that handles exactly that.' This would have added a second hook to the appointment beyond solar alone, making the sit more valuable and the callback more urgent.
